content.problemStatement:
You are given several test cases.
In each test case, you have a list of integers representing cards handhandhand. Your task is to determine if it's possible to arrange all the cards into groups such that:


Each group contains exactly **groupSize** cards.
The cards in every group must form a sequence of **consecutive integers**.
NOTE:Reordering of the array is allowed.

Function DeclarationFunction NamecanGroupcanGroupcanGroup – This function checks whether a given set of cards can be rearranged into groups of a fixed size such that every group consists of **consecutive integers**.

Parameters
handhandhand : An array of integers representing the values written on the cards.
groupSizegroupSizegroupSize : An integer representing the required number of cards in each group.
Return Value
Returns `"true"` if it is possible to divide all cards into groups of size groupSizegroupSizegroupSize such that each group forms a sequence of consecutive integers.
Returns `"false"` otherwise.
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T — the number of test cases. For each test case, the first line contains two integers N and groupSize — the number of cards and the required size of each group. The second line contains N space‑separated integers representing the card values.
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a single line containing "YES" if the cards can be rearranged into groups of size groupSize where each group consists of consecutive integers, otherwise output "NO".

sampleTestCases.explanation: The cards can be grouped as [5,6,7], [6,7,8], [10,11,12]; each group has size 3 and consists of consecutive numbers.
Value 9 cannot be paired with any other card to form a consecutive pair, so grouping is impossible.
All three cards form the single required group [1,2,3].

hidden.label: edge case with duplicates causing failure

solution.approach: For each test case first check if N is divisible by groupSize; if not, answer is NO. Otherwise sort the array. Use a map (or two parallel vectors) to store each distinct value and its remaining count. Iterate over the distinct values in increasing order. While the current value still has count ≥ groupSize, try to form a group starting at this value: for j from 0 to groupSize‑1 check that value+ j exists and has a positive count; if any check fails, answer is NO. Decrease the count of each used value by one. After processing all values, if every count is zero the answer is YES. The algorithm is greedy because the smallest remaining card must belong to a group that starts with it; otherwise it could never be placed in a consecutive group.
solution.timeComplexity: Sorting dominates: O(N log N) per test case. All other operations are linear in the number of distinct values.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(N) for storing the sorted array and the count structures.
